scopeNote: Multi-strategy hedge fund / institutional asset manager. Covers (1) Portfolio Management at pod/strategy level, (2) central portfolio construction and capital allocation (CIO office), and (3) the Investment Committee and investment reporting/decision-record function. Distinguishes regulatory requirements (REG), widely accepted institutional practice (PRACTICE), and firm-specific judgment calls (JUDGMENT) throughout.

departments.name: Portfolio Management (Pod / Strategy Level)
departments.mandate: Generate risk-adjusted returns within a written, delegated mandate set by the CIO office / Investment Committee. The mandate (pod term sheet or strategy IPS) defines the investable universe, allocated capital, gross/net exposure ranges, per-name and concentration limits, factor-neutrality requirements, liquidity constraints, and a drawdown/stop-out schedule. The PM owns day-to-day portfolio construction, position sizing, rebalancing and drawdown management inside those boundaries; the PM does NOT own the limits themselves, which are set and enforced outside the pod.
responsibilities: Idea generation/selection and translation of research into positions with documented rationale

Position sizing: scaling positions by conviction, expected return, volatility, liquidity and marginal risk contribution, within per-name/issuer/sector limits (Kelly-style or vol-target scaling in systematic pods; discretionary conviction-weighting in fundamental pods)
Portfolio construction: maintaining the book's target factor/beta profile (e.g., market-, sector-, factor-neutral where mandated), hedging residual exposures, managing gross/net within bands
Rebalancing: bringing actual weights back to target on a rules-based cadence and/or drift-trigger basis, net of transaction-cost considerations
Drawdown management: monitoring peak-to-trough P&L against the stop-out schedule; proactively de-grossing as triggers approach; executing mandated de-risking on breach
Liquidity and capacity management for the book (days-to-liquidate, borrow availability, crowding)
Compliance with restricted lists, MNPI/information-barrier policies, position-limit regimes (e.g., CFTC/exchange limits, EU short-selling disclosure)
Producing trade rationale, book commentary and review materials; contributing to IC memos and investor communications
Supervising pod analysts/execution traders; certifying accuracy of positions vs shadow records

workflows.name: Daily book review and risk sign-off
workflows.description: PM reviews overnight/flash P&L, exposure and risk reports (VaR, factor exposures, stress results, limit utilization), reconciles positions vs fund accounting/administrator shadow, and adjusts the book. Daily P&L attribution is standard at institutional scale ($1B+ funds overwhelmingly run daily attribution).
workflows.inputs: Start-of-day positions from operations; flash P&L; independent risk report (VaR, factor, stress, limit headroom); market/borrow data; research notes; compliance restricted list
workflows.outputs: Orders to trading/execution; updated target portfolio; PM daily commentary; exception acknowledgements (limit warnings)

workflows.name: Position sizing and trade construction
workflows.description: New or resized positions are constructed from a research thesis: expected return vs downside, marginal contribution to book risk, liquidity (ADV %, days-to-cover), borrow cost, and remaining limit headroom. Systematic pods embed sizing in validated models; discretionary pods document sizing rationale. Positions above delegated thresholds require CIO/IC approval.
workflows.inputs: Research memo/thesis; liquidity and borrow data; limit headroom from risk system; transaction cost estimates
workflows.outputs: Trade tickets with sizing rationale; updated model portfolio; (above threshold) approval request to CIO/IC

workflows.name: Rebalancing
workflows.cadence: Systematic: daily/weekly rules-based. Discretionary: weekly/monthly plus drift-band triggers
workflows.description: Actual vs target weights monitored; rebalancing executed when drift exceeds tolerance bands or on scheduled review, per a formalized rebalancing policy (CFA Institute IPS guidance: rebalancing policy with trigger thresholds and review schedule should be written down, not ad hoc).
workflows.inputs: Target weights/model portfolio; actual positions; drift report; transaction-cost model; corporate-action and flow calendar
workflows.outputs: Rebalancing order list; post-rebalance confirmation; drift-exception log

workflows.name: Drawdown management and de-grossing
workflows.cadence: Continuous monitoring; escalation at defined trigger levels
workflows.description: Peak-to-trough drawdown on allocated capital tracked against the stop-out schedule. Widely reported multi-strategy practice: intervention triggers around 3-5% drawdown (capital cut, often ~50%) and termination/full stop-out around 5-10% (e.g., figures reported for Millennium in practice literature: 5% triggers 50% capital reduction, 7.5% terminates the pod). Enforcement sits with the central risk desk, deliberately designed so no individual can argue past a stop. Exact levels are firm-specific JUDGMENT; the existence of hard, externally enforced triggers is widely accepted PRACTICE.
workflows.inputs: Real-time P&L vs allocated capital; stop-out schedule from mandate; central risk desk notices
workflows.outputs: De-grossing plan and executed risk reduction; escalation notice to CIO/risk; post-event review memo

workflows.name: Monthly portfolio and attribution review with CIO/risk
workflows.description: Review of returns vs benchmark/hurdle, attribution by position/sector/factor, limit utilization, thesis health (are positions performing for the stated reason), capacity, and any breaches or near-misses.
workflows.inputs: Independent performance attribution (ex-post and ex-ante); risk utilization history; breach log
workflows.outputs: Pod review pack; action items (trim/exit lists); input to capital reallocation decisions

workflows.name: Contribution to IC / investor reporting cycle
workflows.description: PM provides commentary, outlook and position-level context for the IC pack, investor letters, and regulatory reporting data (Form PF / AIFMD Annex IV data assembled by operations/compliance from PM books).
workflows.inputs: Attribution and exposure data; templates from reporting team
workflows.outputs: PM commentary; certified position data; responses to allocator due-diligence questions

controls: REG (US): Advisers Act fiduciary duty; Rule 206(4)-7 requires written policies/procedures covering portfolio management processes, trading, and allocation, reviewed at least annually

REG (US): Rule 204-2 books and records - written communications relating to recommendations, advice, orders and performance must be retained (generally 5 years, first 2 easily accessible); this is the statutory hook that makes PM decision rationale auditable

REG (EU): AIFMD Art. 15 and Level 2 - all risks relevant to each AIF's strategy must be identified, measured, monitored with adequate frequency; risk management must be functionally and hierarchically separated from portfolio management (Level 2 Art. 42-45; Art. 80 conditions on delegation); Level 2 Art. 18 requires a written, regularly updated due-diligence policy for investments
REG (UK): FCA SYSC + SM&CR - named senior manager accountability (Statements of Responsibilities) for investment management; duty of responsibility for breaches
REG (US, where applicable): CFTC/exchange speculative position limits; SEC/EU short-position reporting
PRACTICE: Pre-trade compliance checks (restricted list, concentration, mandate rules) embedded in OMS; hard blocks not advisory warnings for prohibited items

PRACTICE: Hard risk limits monitored and enforced by an independent risk function with authority to cut positions (MFA Sound Practices: senior management should not outsource risk monitoring; a consistent risk framework such as VaR should be employed)

PRACTICE: Model governance for systematic sizing/portfolio-construction models by analogy to Fed/OCC/FDIC model risk guidance (SR 11-7, superseded April 17, 2026 by SR 26-2 interagency Revised Guidance on Model Risk Management): development documentation, independent validation, ongoing monitoring
PRACTICE: Trade rationale capture at order entry; decision-to-execution audit trail linking thesis/memo to orders to fills
PRACTICE: Code of ethics / personal account dealing controls (Rule 204A-1 in the US)
JUDGMENT: exact limit schedule (per-name %, gross/net bands, factor limits), stop-out levels, and PM discretion thresholds vary by firm, strategy and liquidity profile
departments.independence: Embedded (first line of defense). PMs sit in the revenue function and cannot be independent; the control design instead requires that risk limit-setting and enforcement, valuation, and performance measurement sit OUTSIDE portfolio management. Under AIFMD this separation is a legal requirement; in the US it is supervisory expectation and near-universal institutional practice.
dependencies: Independent Risk Management (limits, monitoring, stop-out enforcement)
Operations/Fund Accounting (positions, P&L, reconciliation, shadow NAV)
Compliance (restricted lists, personal trading, MNPI barriers, regulatory reporting)
Research & Data platform (internal research, market data, alternative data)

handoffs.content: Orders with limit prices/urgency/constraints and algo instructions; PM receives back fills, execution quality (TCA) reports

Outbound: full position transparency, strategy changes, new instrument requests. Inbound: daily risk reports, limit utilization, breach notices and mandated de-risking instructions (which the PM must execute, not negotiate)
Outbound: performance/attribution packs, capital requests, capacity estimates. Inbound: mandate letter, allocated capital and limit schedule, reallocation and stop-out decisions
Investment memos for above-threshold positions/new strategies; periodic strategy review presentations; responses to IC challenge
Outbound: trade rationale records, communications (all on captured channels), attestations. Inbound: restricted list updates, breach findings, policy changes
Outbound: trade details, booking instructions. Inbound: reconciled positions, P&L, break reports requiring PM confirmation
Monthly/quarterly commentary, DDQ answers, exposure summaries (reviewed by compliance before release)

regulatoryBasis: Investment Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (books and records: recommendations, orders, performance) - 17 CFR 275.204-2
Investment Advisers Act Rule 206(4)-7 (compliance program, annual review)

SEC Form PF (portfolio data: exposures, leverage, liquidity; large hedge fund advisers file quarterly; 72-hour current reports for extraordinary losses/margin events; thresholds under amendment in 2026)
AIFMD Art. 15 + Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 231/2013 Arts. 18 (due diligence), 42-45 (risk management separation), 80 (functional/hierarchical separation on delegation); Annex IV reporting
FCA SYSC 4-10 and SM&CR (SYSC 23-24 allocation of prescribed responsibilities)
CFTC position limits regime (17 CFR Part 150, 2020 final rule) for covered derivatives
Fed/OCC/FDIC SR 26-2 Revised Guidance on Model Risk Management (April 2026, supersedes SR 11-7) - banking guidance applied by analogy to quant model governance
practiceNotes: Multi-manager platforms run PMs as semi-autonomous pods with own P&L; capital is reallocated near-continuously based on performance, drawdown behavior and risk utilization (practice literature; specific mechanics are opaque and vary by firm)

Discretionary pod platforms run risk control sequentially/reactively (central risk desk detects breach, then intervenes); systematic multi-strategy platforms embed position sizing and drawdown control continuously in execution logic (Resonanz Capital analysis) - the research platform should support both patterns
Reported stop-out conventions (3-5% intervention, 5-7.5%+ termination) come from secondary/practice sources, not primary fund disclosure - treat as indicative, not standard

PM artifacts consumed daily: risk report, flash P&L, attribution, borrow/financing report, restricted list. PM artifacts produced daily: orders, commentary, updated model portfolio. This artifact loop is the core of the front-office data model

departments.name: Central Portfolio Construction & Capital Allocation (CIO Office)
departments.mandate: Allocate the firm's capital and risk budget across strategies/pods to maximize firm-level risk-adjusted return, subject to fund-level mandate, leverage, liquidity and investor commitments. Owns the aggregate book: netting across pods, firm-level hedging overlays, factor/crowding management, capacity, and the pod mandate/limit architecture. Functions as an internal capital-allocation business sitting between the pods and the Investment Committee.
responsibilities: Define and issue pod/strategy mandates (universe, capital, limit schedule, stop-out schedule, hurdle/payout terms)

Allocate and dynamically reallocate capital across pods based on realized Sharpe, drawdown behavior, capacity, correlation/crowding, and opportunity set (monthly/weekly tactical shifts are common; firm-level controls prevent any single strategy from dominating the risk budget)
Monitor aggregate exposures that are invisible at pod level (shared factor exposures - rates, dollar, energy, crowding - that cause simultaneous multi-pod drawdowns in macro shocks)
Run firm-level hedging/overlay programs and manage fund-level gross/net leverage with treasury
Sponsor and gate new PM/strategy onboarding (business case, backtest/track-record review, mandate design) for IC approval
Enforce, jointly with independent risk, the stop-out and capital-reduction schedule
Manage capacity and coordinate with IR on inflows/redemptions and their allocation

workflows.name: Capital allocation review
workflows.cadence: Formal monthly/quarterly; tactical adjustments weekly or intra-month
workflows.description: Review pod-level risk-adjusted performance, drawdown state, risk utilization and correlation; decide increases/cuts/terminations. Decisions above defined size or involving new strategies escalate to the IC. Documented in a capital allocation memo with rationale (auditability).
workflows.inputs: Pod P&L and attribution; risk utilization and stress results; factor/crowding analytics; capacity and liquidity estimates; financing costs from treasury; investor flow projections
workflows.outputs: Updated capital allocation schedule; revised pod limit schedules; capital allocation memo (rationale, alternatives considered); termination/stop-out notices

workflows.name: Firm-level risk and stress review
workflows.cadence: Daily monitoring; weekly deep-dive with CRO; monthly to IC
workflows.description: Aggregate exposure, netting and stress review across all pods with independent risk; identify shared exposures and impose top-of-house hedges or pod-level constraints.
workflows.inputs: Aggregated position data; independent risk stress scenarios; counterparty/margin data
workflows.outputs: Overlay hedge orders; top-of-house constraint changes; firm risk summary for IC/board pack

workflows.name: New PM / new strategy onboarding
workflows.description: Business case, due diligence on track record/backtests (model validation for systematic strategies), mandate and limit design, operational readiness sign-offs (risk, compliance, ops, treasury), then IC approval before capital is released.
workflows.inputs: Candidate track record/backtest; proposed mandate; risk/compliance/ops assessments
workflows.outputs: Pod mandate letter/term sheet; IC approval memo and decision record; onboarding checklist completion

workflows.name: Annual mandate re-underwrite
workflows.description: Each pod mandate, limit schedule and payout structure re-reviewed against realized behavior and firm strategy; feeds annual compliance review under Rule 206(4)-7 and AIFMD periodic risk-policy review.
workflows.inputs: Full-year attribution; breach/exception history; peer/capacity analysis
workflows.outputs: Renewed or amended mandates; retirement of stale limits; report to IC

controls: PRACTICE: Documented capital-allocation methodology and decision memos (who decided, on what data, why) - required in substance by fiduciary duty and books-and-records rules even though no rule names 'capital allocation' as such
PRACTICE: Dual control with independent risk on limit changes; limit increases require risk sign-off, not just CIO instruction
PRACTICE: Delegated authority matrix - CIO discretion up to defined size/risk; IC approval above; board/GP notification for material changes to fund-level leverage or strategy mix
PRACTICE: Override log - any override of model-driven or policy-driven allocation must be documented with rationale and reviewed by IC (mirrors SR 26-2 expectations on overrides of model output)

REG (EU): AIFMD risk-profile consistency - the AIFM must ensure each AIF's risk profile corresponds to its size, portfolio structure, strategies and objectives; leverage limits per AIF must be set and reported (Annex IV)
REG (US): Form PF exposures/leverage/liquidity data must reconcile to the allocation and risk records
departments.independence: Embedded in the front office (it is an investment function) but structurally distinct from pod PMs to avoid marking-own-homework on capital decisions; must operate in check-and-balance with a fully independent risk function. In smaller firms this function collapses into the CIO personally - the control then shifts to IC oversight and documentation.
dependencies: Independent Risk Management (aggregate analytics, stress, veto on limit increases)
Performance/analytics team (attribution at pod and firm level)
Investor Relations (flows, capacity commitments, investor-level terms)
Compliance/Legal (mandate documents, side letters, regulatory limits)

handoffs.content: Mandate letters, capital allocations, limit and stop-out schedules, reallocation decisions; receives back performance packs and capital requests
Capital allocation proposals and memos, new-strategy approval requests, firm-level exposure and performance summary; receives back approvals/conditions/rejections recorded in IC minutes
Outbound: proposed limit/mandate changes for independent review. Inbound: aggregate stress results, crowding alerts, breach escalations, stop-out triggers
Outbound: projected financing needs from allocation changes. Inbound: financing costs, margin constraints, counterparty capacity that constrain allocation
Outbound: capacity guidance, strategy-mix narrative for investors. Inbound: subscription/redemption pipeline affecting allocatable capital

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act fiduciary duty (allocation decisions must be in clients'/fund's best interest; cross-fund allocation conflicts must be managed and disclosed)
AIFMD Art. 15 (risk profile), Art. 25 (leverage), Annex IV reporting
Form PF (fund-level leverage, exposure, liquidity reporting)
IOSCO CIS governance and conflicts-of-interest guidance (IOSCOPD219, IOSCOPD342) - independent oversight of operator decisions affecting investors
practiceNotes: Multi-strategy funds operate under unified management with a single committee overseeing capital allocation and risk across strategies; tiered decision structures (senior PMs oversee cross-strategy allocation, strategy leads advise) are typical (CAIS, AlphaMaven primers)

Dynamic reallocation cadence is a differentiator: some platforms shift capital weekly/monthly on Sharpe momentum; others re-underwrite quarterly - the platform should treat allocation cadence as a configurable policy, not a constant
Netting across uncorrelated pods produces trading-cost and performance-fee efficiencies (pass-through vs netted fee structures differ by firm)
This function is the least standardized and least publicly documented of the three - primary sources are thin; most detail comes from allocator research and practice literature

departments.name: Investment Committee & Investment Reporting
departments.mandate: Formal governance body (plus its supporting secretariat/reporting function) that authorizes investment decisions above delegated thresholds, approves new strategies/mandates and exceptions, oversees performance against mandate, and ensures decisions and their rationale are documented, challenged and auditable. It is the mechanism by which fiduciary 'procedural prudence' is demonstrated: a documented, consistently followed process with recorded deliberation, votes and dissent.
responsibilities: Approve/reject above-threshold positions, new strategies/pods, illiquid or hard-to-value (Level 3) investments, exceptions and overrides to policy
Set and periodically review the delegated authority matrix (what PMs/CIO can do without IC approval)
Review performance, risk, limit utilization/breaches, liquidity and counterparty summaries at each meeting
Maintain the IC charter (composition, quorum, voting rules, conflicts procedure) and review it annually
Maintain decision records: memos, minutes, votes, dissents, conditions of approval, and a decisions log reconciled to executed trades
Oversee the production and integrity of investment reporting (IC packs, board reporting, investor reporting inputs, GIPS-compliant performance where compliance is claimed)
Conduct annual self-assessment and post-mortems on major decisions (wins and losses)

workflows.name: IC meeting cycle
workflows.cadence: Formal monthly or quarterly; weekly pipeline/screening meetings in deal-driven strategies; ad hoc sessions for urgent approvals or breach escalations
workflows.description: Standing agenda: prior minutes approval, conflicts declarations, performance/risk review, pending approvals (memos pre-circulated), watchlist, exceptions/breaches, action-item follow-up. Materials distributed in advance so meeting time is spent on challenge, not presentation (Northern Trust / CAPTRUST IC best-practice guidance). Institutional committees are typically 5-9 members with an odd number to prevent ties; hedge fund ICs are commonly smaller (3-5) and CIO-chaired - composition is JUDGMENT, documented process is PRACTICE.
workflows.inputs: IC pack (performance vs benchmark, attribution, risk dashboard, limit/breach log, liquidity, counterparty); investment memos; prior minutes and open action items
workflows.outputs: Approved minutes; decision records with votes and recorded dissent; conditions/covenants attached to approvals; updated decisions log; action items

workflows.name: Investment memo preparation and challenge
workflows.description: Standardized memo template: thesis and edge; expected return and downside scenarios; proposed sizing and limits; liquidity/exit analysis; key risks and mitigants; valuation basis; relevant regulatory/ESG considerations; explicit exit/review criteria. Memos capture the information, expectations and logic behind the decision (Addepar/Stanford research on investment memos) and create the reasonable-basis record required by CFA Standard V(A) and, in the EU, the written due-diligence policy under AIFMD Level 2 Art. 18. Version-controlled; the version voted on is preserved.
workflows.inputs: PM/analyst research; risk pre-review; compliance/legal review for restricted or conflicted items
workflows.outputs: Final memo of record; challenge log (questions raised and responses); approval/rejection with rationale

workflows.name: Decision recording and dissent capture
workflows.description: Minutes record attendees, quorum, materials referenced, key discussion points, questions and objections, the vote, and any dissent. ERISA-influenced fiduciary guidance is explicit: minutes are the evidence of a prudent process ('if a prudent decision was made and no one documented it, did it happen?'), and should reflect in detail why an investment was included or rejected. CFA Standard V(A) guidance: a committee may issue a decision an individual disagrees with if it has a reasonable and adequate basis; dissenting members can have their names disassociated - recording dissent rather than suppressing it is the sound practice. In practice most votes are unanimous because committees discuss to consensus; the record must still show the deliberation.
workflows.inputs: Meeting deliberation; draft minutes; voting results
workflows.outputs: Approved minutes (approved at next meeting); dissent register; decisions log entry linking memo -> decision -> subsequent execution (decision-to-trade audit trail)

workflows.name: Approval-threshold administration
workflows.cadence: Continuous; matrix reviewed at least annually
workflows.description: Delegated authority matrix defines what requires IC approval: typically new strategies/pods, positions above X% of NAV or above per-name limits, illiquid/side-pocket investments, exceptions to the IPS/mandate, cross trades and principal transactions (which also carry statutory consent requirements), and overrides of risk-driven stop-outs. Thresholds vary by liquidity and firm size - liquid hedge fund strategies delegate more to PMs with hard risk limits; private-markets and allocator ICs approve individual deals. Threshold design is JUDGMENT; having a written matrix with escalation triggers is PRACTICE.
workflows.inputs: Charter and authority matrix; requests exceeding delegated authority; compliance flags
workflows.outputs: Approvals/rejections/conditional approvals; amended matrix; exception log

workflows.name: Investment reporting production and distribution
workflows.cadence: Monthly IC pack; quarterly board/investor reporting; annual GIPS and regulatory cycle
workflows.description: Reporting function assembles the IC pack and downstream reporting: performance vs benchmark, attribution, risk summary, limit utilization, breach/exception reporting, liquidity and counterparty exposure. Feeds: board reporting, investor letters, Form PF (large hedge fund advisers quarterly within 60 days of quarter end, plus 72-hour current reports on trigger events), AIFMD Annex IV, and GIPS composite reports where the firm claims compliance (GIPS 2020: pooled funds need not be in composites unless the strategy is offered as a segregated account; time-weighted return reports build to 10 years of history; independent verification is optional but common).
workflows.inputs: Certified performance data from fund accounting/performance team; risk data; PM commentary; compliance sign-off
workflows.outputs: IC pack; board pack; investor reporting inputs; regulatory filings data; GIPS composite reports and verification support

workflows.name: Annual governance review and post-mortems
workflows.description: Charter/IPS review; committee self-assessment; review of decision quality (hit rate of approved vs rejected proposals, post-mortems on large losses AND large wins); feeds the Rule 206(4)-7 annual compliance review record.
workflows.inputs: Decisions log; performance outcomes; breach and exception history
workflows.outputs: Updated charter/IPS; self-assessment record; process-change actions

controls: PRACTICE: Written IC charter: composition, quorum, voting rules (majority vs chair-decides vs CIO-veto - varies), conflicts-of-interest procedure (declarations at meeting start, recusal), and delegated authority matrix
PRACTICE: Pre-circulated materials with a standard memo template; no approval without a memo of record

PRACTICE: Minutes drafted promptly, approved at next meeting, retained in an immutable/auditable store; decisions log reconciled to trades so any position can be traced back to an authorizing decision
PRACTICE: Dissent and objections recorded, not smoothed over; conditions of approval tracked to completion

REG (US): Rule 204-2 retention applies to memos, minutes and related communications (5+ years); Rule 206(4)-7 annual review must cover the decision-making process; SEC examiners request IC minutes and decision rationale in exams

REG (US, ERISA-touching mandates): fiduciary prudence is judged on process, not outcomes - substantive prudence (expertise) plus procedural prudence (robust, documented, consistently followed process); DOL 2026 proposed guidance on selecting designated investment alternatives reinforces process documentation

REG (UK): SM&CR - collective committee decisions do not dilute individual senior manager accountability; the FCA expects clear lines of accountability and adequate MI to governance bodies (2025 asset management portfolio letter)
REG (EU): AIFMD Level 2 Art. 18 written due-diligence policy; Art. 60+ governance and senior-management responsibilities

PRACTICE (voluntary standard): GIPS for performance presentation; CFA Institute Code and Standards V(A) (diligence/reasonable basis), V(B) (communication), V(C) (record retention: maintain records supporting investment analyses and recommendations)
departments.independence: The IC is a governance body of the first line (investment leadership), not an independent control function. Independence is achieved by: (a) mandatory participation/visibility of the CRO and CCO (voting membership for the CRO varies by firm; compliance is usually a non-voting observer); (b) the fund's board of directors (with independent directors, per AIMA offshore fund governance practice) sitting above the manager's IC with ultimate fiduciary responsibility for the fund vehicle; (c) the immutable decision record enabling ex-post challenge by compliance, auditors, regulators and investors.
dependencies: Risk Management (independent risk opinion on every above-threshold proposal; risk dashboard for packs)
Compliance/Legal (conflicts screening, regulatory sign-off, records retention)
Performance/Fund Accounting (certified performance and attribution data)
Fund Board / GP (matters reserved above the IC: material strategy changes, valuation policy, side pockets, gates)

handoffs.content: Inbound: memos, capital allocation proposals, exception requests. Outbound: approvals with conditions, rejections with rationale, revised delegated authority - all as formal decision records
Inbound: independent risk opinion on proposals, breach escalations requiring IC action. Outbound: risk-appetite and limit-framework endorsements, mandated remediations

Inbound: conflicts flags, regulatory-change items requiring IC awareness. Outbound: minutes/decision records for the compliance archive and annual review; exception approvals for compliance monitoring
Quarterly summary of decisions, performance vs mandate, breaches and remediation; escalation of matters reserved for the board (valuation disputes, gates/side pockets, material strategy drift)

Approved performance and commentary for investor letters and DDQs; decision narratives for allocator operational due diligence (allocators routinely test whether IC minutes exist and match stated process)
On request: charter, minutes, memos, decisions log, dissent register - the auditability package demonstrating that stated process was followed

regulatoryBasis: No US statute mandates an investment committee per se - the obligation is indirect via Advisers Act fiduciary duty, Rule 206(4)-7 (documented process) and Rule 204-2 (records of recommendations/advice)
ERISA prudence caselaw and DOL guidance (process-based prudence) for any ERISA-plan mandates; drives documentation norms industry-wide
FCA SYSC/SM&CR: governance-body effectiveness, MI adequacy, named accountability (SYSC 23-24; FCA asset management portfolio letter expectations)
AIFMD Level 2 Arts. 18, 57-66 (due diligence, organizational/governance requirements); Annex IV reporting
IOSCO Examination of Governance for Collective Investment Schemes (IOSCOPD219) and conflicts guidelines (IOSCOPD342)
GIPS standards (voluntary; CFA Institute) for composite construction and performance presentation; hedge fund adoption is lower than long-only but rising with institutionalization
CFA Institute Standards of Professional Conduct V(A)-(C) (reasonable basis, communication, record retention; group-decision dissent handling)
practiceNotes: Formality varies sharply by firm type: multi-strategy pod platforms concentrate authority in the CIO/capital-allocation process with a thin formal IC (speed matters; risk limits do the governance work), while allocators, endowments, OCIOs and private-markets firms run formal voting ICs with full memo cycles. The research platform should model 'IC' as a configurable governance layer, not a fixed org unit
Memo quality practice (Addepar/Stanford brief): lead with the decision requested; capture expectations and logic explicitly so post-mortems can distinguish bad process from bad luck

Auditability pattern that examiners and allocator ODD teams test: memo (version of record) -> minutes (deliberation, vote, dissent) -> decisions log -> executed trades -> performance attribution; any break in that chain is a finding

Dissent recording is genuinely contested in practice: some GCs advise minimal minutes (litigation exposure), while fiduciary-governance consultants advise detailed minutes including objections; the dominant institutional position post-ERISA-litigation-wave is detailed process documentation

structuralRecommendations: Split 'Portfolio Management' into two modeled functions: pod/strategy-level PM and a central CIO-office capital-allocation & portfolio-construction function. In a multi-strategy context these have different mandates, artifacts, cadences and controls, and the capital-allocation layer is where cross-pod decisions (the scope's 'capital allocation across strategies/pods') actually live.

Model the Investment Committee as a governance layer/committee with a charter, not an operating department; its only operating staff is a secretariat. Make its formality configurable (thin CIO-led approval process for liquid multi-strat vs full memo-and-vote IC for private markets/allocator use cases).

Move production of performance/investor reporting OUT of the IC construct into an independent performance & reporting function (middle office/fund accounting ownership, GIPS responsibility there); the IC consumes certified reporting and must not produce the numbers it is overseeing.

Keep Risk Management strictly outside Portfolio Management in the department graph - functional/hierarchical separation is a hard AIFMD requirement and universal institutional expectation; limit enforcement and stop-outs must be modeled as risk-owned controls that PMs receive, not set.

Add an explicit cross-cutting 'decision record / audit trail' capability linking memo -> IC decision (vote + dissent) -> mandate/limit change -> orders -> fills -> attribution; auditability is the core regulatory hook (Rule 204-2 / 206(4)-7, AIFMD Art. 18) and the thing allocator ODD teams test.

Add a model-governance sub-function (shared between Risk and the quant platform) for position-sizing/portfolio-construction models, referencing the interagency model risk framework (SR 26-2, which superseded SR 11-7 in April 2026 - do not cite SR 11-7 as current).
disagreementsAndVariation: IC formality: hedge fund practice literature shows multi-strat platforms governing via CIO + risk limits with minimal formal IC, whereas fiduciary/consultant literature (Northern Trust, CAPTRUST, Mercer, Cambridge Associates) assumes formal voting committees. Both are real; varies by firm type, strategy liquidity and investor base.

Drawdown/stop-out numbers: widely repeated figures (e.g., Millennium 5% -> 50% capital cut, 7.5% -> termination; 3-5% intervention elsewhere) come from secondary practice sources (substack/industry blogs), not primary disclosure. The existence of hard externally-enforced triggers is well supported; the specific levels are indicative only.

Dissent recording: consultant/fiduciary guidance recommends detailed minutes including objections; some legal advisers counsel leaner minutes to limit litigation discovery exposure. CFA Standards permit disassociation from group conclusions. No single standard exists - firms make a documented policy choice.

CRO role on the IC: some firms give the CRO a vote (or veto), others keep risk as challenger/observer to preserve independence; AIFMD separation requirements push EU firms toward the observer/veto model.

GIPS adoption: effectively standard for institutional long-only managers, still a minority practice among hedge funds (ACA/CFA Institute materials note the 2020 standards were redesigned partly to make pooled-fund/hedge-fund compliance feasible).

Form PF requirements are in flux (2026): current rules require large hedge fund advisers (>= $1.5B hedge fund AUM) to file quarterly within 60 days plus 72-hour current reports; 2026 SEC/CFTC 'burden-reduction' proposals would raise the large-adviser threshold to $10B and slim reporting; latest compliance deadline moved to October 1, 2026. Any platform encoding of Form PF logic should be flagged as unstable.

Model risk guidance: SR 11-7 was superseded by interagency SR 26-2 on April 17, 2026; SR 26-2 explicitly scopes OUT generative/agentic AI models and emphasizes risk-based tailoring. It is banking guidance - applied to asset managers only by analogy/institutional convention, which itself varies by firm.

Approval thresholds: liquid-strategy firms delegate heavily to PMs within hard risk limits (IC approves mandates, not trades); private-markets and allocator ICs approve individual investments. Threshold levels (as % NAV or absolute) are firm-specific judgment with no regulatory floor.

sourcesConsulted.whatItSupported: SEC Rule 204-2 books-and-records: retention of written communications relating to recommendations, advice, orders and performance - the statutory basis for auditable PM/IC decision records (consulted via search excerpt of the eCFR text)
Rule 204-2 retention periods (5 years, first 2 easily accessible) and scope of records tied to recommendations/performance
Rule 206(4)-7 elements: written policies covering portfolio management processes, annual review, CCO designation; 204-2 amendment requiring retention of policies and annual-review records
SR 26-2 (April 17, 2026) Revised Guidance on Model Risk Management supersedes SR 11-7 and SR 21-8; risk-based tailoring; generative/agentic AI out of scope
Interagency nature of the 2026 model-risk revision and its relevance thresholds (banking organizations >$30B); confirms SR 11-7 no longer current
AIFMD risk-management framework: identify/document/measure/monitor all relevant risks with adequate frequency; risk profile must correspond to AIF size/structure/strategy
AIFMD functional and hierarchical separation of portfolio management from risk management, incl. Level 2 Art. 80 conditions on delegation
ESMA Q&A on AIFMD application (risk management, delegation, reporting) - authority for EU-side requirements

AIMA Guides to Sound Practices catalogue, incl. Guide for European Hedge Fund Managers covering the investment process, portfolio risk management and operational controls; AIMA position that fund governance rests with the fund board, which delegates to the manager

MFA Sound Practices for Hedge Fund Managers: risk monitoring must not be outsourced by senior management; consistent portfolio risk framework (e.g., VaR); seven-section structure covering risk management and trading operations
GIPS composite-construction requirements (all discretionary fee-paying portfolios in composites) and firm-level reporting obligations
2020 GIPS changes relevant to hedge funds: pooled funds need not be in composites unless offered as segregated accounts; valuation-frequency implications; verification practice
IOSCO Examination of Governance for Collective Investment Schemes - independent oversight framework above the manager
IOSCO guidelines on conflicts of interest facing CIS operators - basis for conflicts declarations and independent review in investment decision processes
FCA SM&CR overview: Statements of Responsibilities, senior manager duty of responsibility - individual accountability overlaying committee decisions
FCA allocation of prescribed senior-management responsibilities (SYSC 24)
FCA supervisory expectations for asset managers: governance effectiveness, senior accountability for risk control, adequate MI to governance bodies
Form PF current-reporting amendments: 72-hour current reports for large hedge fund advisers on extraordinary losses, margin/default events, PB terminations, redemption events
2026 SEC/CFTC Form PF amendment proposal (burden reduction; threshold changes) - evidence requirements are in flux
Analysis of 2026 Form PF direction incl. proposed large-hedge-fund-adviser threshold increase from $1.5B to $10B
CFA Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis - group/committee research decisions, reasonable-basis documentation, and dissenting-member disassociation
IPS elements: risk/return objectives, constraints, benchmark, formalized rebalancing policy with drift tolerances and review schedule, governance procedures
Investment memos capture information, expectations and logic behind decisions; decisions should lead the document (direct fetch returned 403; content taken from search excerpt - flagged as such)

IC organization best practices: charter, composition, record-keeping; minutes covering agenda items, discussion points, questions/objections and final decisions (consulted via search excerpts; companion 'running an effective meeting' PDF fetch timed out)
IC practice literature: vote all items even with dissenters; minutes content; facilitation to surface dissent productively
ERISA fiduciary documentation: minutes as evidence of prudent process; record in detail why investments were included or rejected
Fiduciary committee governance: substantive vs procedural prudence; committees, IPS, regular reviews, detailed minutes
DOL 2026 proposal on fiduciary duties in selecting investment alternatives - current regulatory reinforcement of process documentation

Fetched in full: discretionary pod platforms as internal capital-allocation businesses with sequential/reactive central risk enforcement vs systematic platforms with sizing/drawdown control embedded in execution logic; cross-pod shared-exposure drawdown risk
Pod-model mechanics: per-pod risk budgets as max drawdown, automatic capital cuts on breach, reported Millennium stop-out levels (secondary practice source - flagged as indicative, not authoritative)
Multi-strategy funds operate under unified management with a single committee overseeing capital allocation and risk across strategies

Tiered decision structures (senior PMs oversee cross-strategy allocation; strategy leads advise); dynamic monthly/weekly reallocation on risk-adjusted performance; firm-level risk-budget caps per strategy
Netting benefits across uncorrelated internal strategies (trading-cost and fee efficiencies)
Approval-threshold design considerations: thresholds vary by liquidity/risk profile; threshold flexibility and periodic review (practice commentary - low authority, used for design patterns only)
Fetched: confirms multi-strategy funds are heterogeneous in business model, risk parameters and allocation approach - supports 'varies by firm' framing (explicitly lacks operational specifics)
